main_sections.title: One Thing at a Time
main_sections.poem_text:
Work while you work,
Play while you play;
To be useful and happy,
That is the way.

All that you do,
Do with all your might;
Things done by halves,
Are never done right.

One thing at a time,
And that done well;
Is a very good rule,
As many can tell.

Moments should never,
Be trifled away;
So, work while you work,
And play while you play.
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: The poem is a short didactic verse on the importance of focus, effort, and balance. Its central theme is that meaningful work and joyful play are both valuable — but only when done with full attention and wholehearted effort. The tone is gently instructive and encouraging, like a wise elder's advice. The dominant idea is expressed through the recurring contrast of 'work' and 'play', which becomes a refrain that frames the poem. The poet warns against half-hearted efforts ('things done by halves / are never done right') and against wasting time ('moments should never be trifled away'). The poem uses simple end rhyme (play/way, might/right, well/tell, away/play) and a consistent four-line stanza structure that mirrors its message of orderly, steady practice.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
A. Answer the following.
1. Why is it important to focus on doing one thing at a time?
2. Make a list of words that rhyme in the poem 'One thing at a time'.
3. How do you feel after finishing a task that you have done well?
4. What are some of the things you do every day that help you stay calm and focused?

question_bank.stem: Why is it important to focus on doing one thing at a time?
Make a list of words that rhyme in the poem 'One thing at a time'.
How do you feel after finishing a task that you have done well?
What are some of the things you do every day that help you stay calm and focused?

oracy.section_name: Let us Think + Let us Speak

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
B. Think and discuss.
What do you like doing in your free time?

question_bank.stem: What do you like doing in your free time?

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Describe what everyone is doing in the picture. Now use the words and frame meaningful sentences.
[Madhubani-style painting showing many people engaged in different activities simultaneously — farming, dancing, playing, working, cooking, climbing, painting, swimming.]
[Table provided: Who | Is/Are | Doing What (Action). Examples: two girls are going to school; a woman is typing on a computer.]
Using the above sentence structure, create sentences of your own. Write at least seven sentences in your notebook.

visual_stimulus.brief_description: Detailed Madhubani folk-art style painting showing numerous people and animals engaged in diverse simultaneous activities — farming, dancing, playing musical instruments, cooking, climbing, painting, swimming, flying kites — used as a prompt for present continuous sentence making.

word_work.section_name: Let us Learn + Let us Listen

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
A. Match the words with their meanings.
1. might  •  helpful or beneficial
2. useful  •  short periods of time
3. moments  •  strength or power

The case of missing 'l'. Your teacher will read aloud a few words to you. Circle the words in which you don't hear the 'l' sound.
[Word list: leaf, calm, pale, jail, palm, hall, clean, calf, shelf, chalk, talk, silk, balm, plum, self]
tasks_verbatim.transcript_text: Teacher reads aloud the following 15 words one by one: leaf, calm, pale, jail, palm, hall, clean, calf, shelf, chalk, talk, silk, balm, plum, self. Students listen and circle the words where the letter 'l' is silent (not pronounced). Silent-l words in the list: calm, palm, calf, chalk, talk, balm.

question_bank.stem: Circle all words in which you do NOT hear the 'l' sound: leaf, calm, pale, jail, palm, hall, clean, calf, shelf, chalk, talk, silk, balm, plum, self.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Write down your daily routine in the space given below. Tick (✓) if they are work or play.
[Table with columns: Time | What I Do | Work | Play — 6 blank rows for students to fill in their own daily activities and classify each as work or play.]

Now write the above routine as a paragraph.
[Three structured paragraph frames provided:]
My Routine: I begin my day at ......... a.m. I ......... and then ......... . After this, I ......... . By ......... a.m., I am ready for ......... . My mother helps me to ......... and then I leave for school at ......... .
At School: My school starts at ......... a.m./p.m. I have ......... periods before recess. When the time on the big clock reads ......... , the students goes to the playground and play many games together. The school ends at ......... p.m.
At Home: [2 open lines for students to write about their after-school routine.]

A. Guess the following riddles.
Riddle 1: Thirty white horses / On a red hill / Now they chomp / Now they stomp / Now they stand still
Riddle 2: Tall lady / In a white frock, / With a yellow face, / And a red nose. / The longer she stands, / The shorter she grows.
Riddle 3: Hard and brown outside / Soft and white inside / Who am I?

question_bank.stem: Thirty white horses / On a red hill / Now they chomp / Now they stomp / Now they stand still — What am I?
Tall lady / In a white frock, / With a yellow face, / And a red nose. / The longer she stands, / The shorter she grows — What am I?
Hard and brown outside / Soft and white inside / Who am I?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: B. Use the space to draw activities that you enjoy doing in holidays. You may also paste pictures from old newspapers or magazines.
